claims.claim: Epoch AI (Cottier et al.) estimates the amortized hardware-plus-energy cost of the most expensive publicly announced final training runs grew 2.4x per year since 2016 (90% CI 2.0-2.9x), with the cloud-rental method showing 2.6x/year at roughly twice the absolute level.
Epoch AI estimates GPT-4's final training run cost about $40M amortized hardware+energy and Gemini Ultra about $30M; including experiment compute, GPT-4's amortized hardware rises to about $90M.
Stanford AI Index (2024/2025 editions, with Epoch) estimates GPT-4 used $78-79M of compute at cloud-rental prices, Gemini Ultra $191M, and Llama 3.1 405B $170M.
Epoch AI projects that at the 2.4x/year trend, the most expensive publicly announced model by early 2027 will cost about $1 billion for a single training run.
Epoch AI estimates xAI's Grok 4 final training run consumed roughly $490M of compute (~246M H100-hours), excluding labor, experiments, and inference — the largest run Epoch has priced.

DeepSeek-V3's technical report states total training consumed 2.788M H800 GPU-hours (2.664M pre-training on a 2,048-GPU H800 cluster over less than two months) costing $5.576M at an assumed $2/H800-hour, explicitly excluding 'the costs associated with prior research and ablation experiments on architectures, algorithms, or data.'

SemiAnalysis estimates DeepSeek's cumulative GPU investments exceed $500M (~50,000 Hopper-generation GPUs, seeded by High-Flyer's pre-controls purchase of 10,000 A100s), with ~$1.6B server capex and ~$944M operating cost.

Dario Amodei wrote that Claude 3.5 Sonnet 'cost a few $10M's to train,' that frontier capability gets roughly 4x cheaper per year, and that DeepSeek-V3's cost position versus 7-10-month-older US models is 'on-trend at best.'

Dario Amodei said on the In Good Company podcast (mid-2024): 'Right now, 100 million. There are models in training today that are more like a billion,' and predicted $10-100B training costs by 2025-2027.
Sam Altman, asked at an MIT event whether GPT-4 cost $100M to train, answered 'It's more than that.'

Epoch AI's development-cost decomposition across GPT-3, OPT-175B, GPT-4 and Gemini Ultra: hardware 47-67% of total, R&D staff 29-49% (including equity), energy 2-6%; GPT-4's credited contributors rose to 284 vs 25 for GPT-3, and Gemini listed 941 with the highest staff share.

Meta's Llama 3 herd paper records 466 job interruptions over a 54-day snapshot of the 405B pre-training run (419 unexpected, ~78% hardware-related) on up to 16,000 H100 GPUs, with >90% effective training time.

Meta offered AI researchers pay packages reported at $100M during its 2025 superintelligence recruiting push; Bloomberg reported Ruoming Pang's Meta package at over $200M; Altman said Meta tried and failed to poach OpenAI staff with $100M signing bonuses.

OpenAI's leaked audited financials (reported June 2026) show R&D expense of $19.18B in 2025 vs $7.81B in 2024, revenue of $13.07B in 2025 vs $3.7B in 2024, and operating loss of $20.92B in 2025 vs $8.78B in 2024.

OpenAI documents reported by NYT (Sep 2024): ~$5B loss on $3.7B revenue in 2024 with $11.6B revenue projected for 2025; The Information (Jul 2024): ~$7B/year on training+inference compute; Anthropic expected to spend >$2.7B in 2024 of which ~$2.5B compute, with ~$800M annualized revenue by year-end.

OpenAI has reset its compute-spending target to roughly $600B by 2030 (down from $1.4T of announced commitments), projects >$280B revenue in 2030, and is raising >$100B at a $730B pre-money valuation; 2025 revenue was $13.1B with $8B cash burn.

OpenAI's announced compute commitments include the $500B Stargate program (Jan 2025, $100B deploying immediately), a $300B Oracle cloud contract (~5 years, Sep 2025), and a $38B 7-year AWS deal (Nov 2025).

Anthropic raised $3.5B Series E at $61.5B post-money (Mar 2025), $13B Series F at $183B post-money (Sep 2025), then ~$30B (Feb 2026) and ~$65B (May 2026) rounds — roughly $110B across four rounds in fifteen months.
Anthropic's disclosed annualized revenue run rate climbed from ~$1B at the start of 2025 to >$5B (Aug 2025), ~$9B (end 2025), $14B (Feb 2026), >$30B (Apr 2026) and $47B (May 2026).

Anthropic's October 2025 Google deal expands to up to one million TPUs, 'worth tens of billions of dollars,' bringing well over a gigawatt of capacity online in 2026; Amazon remains its primary training partner (Project Rainier).

Hyperscaler capex: Meta spent $72.22B in FY2025 and guided to $115-135B for FY2026 (citing Meta Superintelligence Labs); Alphabet spent $91.45B in 2025 and guided to $175-185B for 2026; Amazon spent $131.8B in 2025 with ~$200B expected in 2026; Microsoft added $80.1B of property and equipment in the first nine months of FY2026.

Anthropic's leaked 2023 fundraising deck targeted up to $5B over two years and stated 'Claude-Next' — 10x more capable than the most powerful AI — would require 'a billion dollars in spending over the next 18 months' on clusters of 'tens of thousands of GPUs' (~10^25 FLOP).

Anthropic's March 2025 OSTP submission projects that by 2027 training a single frontier AI model will require networked computing clusters drawing approximately five gigawatts, and recommends a national target of 50 additional GW of AI-dedicated power by 2027.

Aschenbrenner's 'Racing to the Trillion-Dollar Cluster' projects cluster scale from ~$500M/10MW (GPT-4-class, 2022) to ~$10s of billions/1GW (2026), $100s of billions/10GW (2028) and $1T+/~100GW by 2030, with total AI investment north of $1T annually by 2027.

Time-to-frontier: Anthropic (founded early 2021) shipped Claude 3 Opus March 2024 (~3 years), Opus 4 May 2025, Opus 4.5 November 2025; Mistral shipped Mistral Large ~10 months after its April 2023 founding; DeepSeek shipped V3 ~19 months after its May 2023 spin-off; xAI shipped Grok-3 ~23 months after its March 2023 incorporation.

xAI's Colossus cluster in Memphis — 100,000 H100 GPUs — was built in 122 days with 19 days from first rack to first training run, against a conventional ~3-year datacenter planning horizon; it was later doubled to 200,000 GPUs.

Epoch AI's scaling analysis finds training compute growing ~4x/year and a 2e29 FLOP training run (~5,000x Llama 3.1 405B, requiring ~6 GW) feasible by 2030 if investors commit hundreds of billions of dollars.

Synthesis (ARA estimate): as of mid-2026 a single frontier final training run costs roughly $0.5-1B of compute; a full frontier development program runs in the low single-digit billions per year; staying at the frontier institutionally requires $10B+/year of committed capital.
